metadata.summary: Prevent cache stampede (thundering herd) with three patterns: distributed lock via Redis SETNX, probabilistic early refresh, and request coalescing. Includes production-safe stampede-safe wrapper.

content.title: Cache Stampede Prevention: Distributed Lock, Probabilistic Refresh, Coalescing
content.summary: A cache stampede (thundering herd) occurs when a popular cache key expires and multiple concurrent requests simultaneously attempt to reload it from the origin. Without a guard, all requests hit the database simultaneously, causing a load spike that can cascade into a full outage. Three patterns prevent this: distributed lock (one process rebuilds, others wait), probabilistic early refresh (rebuild before expiry with increasing probability), and request coalescing (in-flight deduplication).

why.p: The README's basic cache-aside decorator has no stampede guard. Under normal traffic this goes unnoticed; during traffic spikes or after cache flushes, the first TTL expiry of a popular key can send hundreds or thousands of concurrent requests to the origin database simultaneously. The origin, sized for cache-hit traffic, cannot absorb this load and the latency spike cascades into timeouts and circuit-breaker trips.

Cache stampedes are especially dangerous because they are self-amplifying: the overloaded origin takes longer to respond, which causes request queues to grow, which causes more timeout retries, which adds further load. The fix must be in the cache layer, not the origin.

list.item: Any cache key that is accessed by more than ~10 concurrent requests per second and has an expensive origin load (slow query, external API call, ML inference).
Keys with a TTL shorter than the origin load time multiplied by peak concurrency — the danger zone where multiple requests can land in the window between expiry and repopulation.
After a cache flush or cold start, when the entire keyspace is empty and all requests are simultaneous cache misses.
Materialized views or aggregation results that take seconds to compute.
Low-concurrency endpoints (single-digit concurrent requests) — lock overhead exceeds stampede risk.
Very fast origin loads (<5ms) — the stampede window is too small to cause measurable damage.
High-cardinality keys where each key is accessed infrequently — no stampede risk by definition.
Probabilistic early refresh on keys where stale serving is never acceptable — early refresh serves slightly stale data during the refresh window.

section.name: distributed-lock
section.p: The distributed lock pattern uses Redis SETNX (SET NX EX) to elect one process as the cache rebuilder. Other concurrent requests either wait briefly and read the repopulated value, or fall back to loading directly from the origin if the lock holder is too slow. The lock key must have a TTL to auto-release on crash.

rule.statement: Distributed cache locks MUST use SET NX EX (atomic set-if-not-exists with expiry) — never a two-step SETNX then EXPIRE. Lock release MUST use a Lua script for atomic check-and-delete to prevent releasing a lock owned by another process after TTL expiry.
rule.rationale: A two-step SETNX + EXPIRE has a crash window between the two commands where the lock is held without a TTL — permanent deadlock. The Lua atomic check-and-delete prevents a slow process from deleting the lock that a faster process already reacquired after TTL expiry.

example.title: Stampede-safe cache wrapper with distributed lock (Python)
example.code:
import time import json import secrets import redis
r = redis.Redis(decode_responses=True)
def cached(key, ttl, fetch, lock_ttl=10, jitter=0.1): """ Cache-aside with stampede prevention via distributed lock.
Args: key: Cache key ttl: Time to live in seconds fetch: Callable that loads data from origin lock_ttl: Lock expiry seconds (must be > expected fetch duration) jitter: TTL jitter fraction (0.1 = ±10%) """ val = r.get(key) if val is not None: return json.loads(val)
lock_key = f"lock:{key}" token = secrets.token_hex(8)
# Atomic SET NX EX — acquire lock if r.set(lock_key, token, nx=True, ex=lock_ttl): try: fresh = fetch() ttl_jitter = int(ttl * (1 + jitter * (secrets.randbelow(200) - 100) / 100)) r.set(key, json.dumps(fresh), ex=ttl_jitter) return fresh finally: # Atomic check-and-delete — release only if still our lock lua_script = """ if redis.call("get", KEYS[1]) == ARGV[1] then return redis.call("del", KEYS[1]) else return 0 end """ r.eval(lua_script, 1, lock_key, token)
# Lost the race; wait briefly and read for _ in range(20): time.sleep(0.05) # 50ms × 20 = 1s max wait val = r.get(key) if val is not None: return json.loads(val) return fetch() # Fallback: load ourselves if lock holder is stuck
example.note: Lock TTL must exceed the expected fetch duration. If fetch takes 2s, lock_ttl=10 gives a 5× safety margin. The fallback fetch() at the end ensures correctness even if the lock holder crashes.

example.title: Distributed lock context manager (Python)

section.name: probabilistic-early-refresh
section.p: Probabilistic early refresh proactively rebuilds a cache entry before its TTL expires. The probability of refreshing increases as the key approaches expiry, following the XFetch algorithm. The key advantage over distributed locks is that the cache always serves a valid (possibly slightly stale) value — there is no blocking wait.

rule.statement: Probabilistic early refresh MUST only be used for entities where serving a value that is at most one TTL period stale is explicitly acceptable. The beta parameter MUST be tuned: beta=1.0 is conservative (refresh in last 30% of TTL), beta=2.0+ is aggressive (starts refreshing at 50% of TTL remaining).
rule.rationale: Early refresh returns the current (slightly stale) cached value while asynchronously updating it. For entities with strict consistency requirements (financial data, inventory), this is incorrect behavior regardless of how small the stale window is.

example.title: Probabilistic early refresh / XFetch (Python)
example.code:
import random import json import redis
class EarlyRefreshCache: def __init__(self, redis_client: redis.Redis): self.redis = redis_client
def get_with_refresh( self, key: str, loader, ttl: int = 3600, beta: float = 1.0 ): """ Get value with probabilistic early refresh (XFetch algorithm).
Args: key: Cache key loader: Function to load data from origin ttl: Full time to live in seconds beta: Refresh aggressiveness (1.0 = standard, 2.0 = aggressive) """ cached_data = self.redis.get(key)
if cached_data: remaining_ttl = self.redis.ttl(key)
if remaining_ttl > 0: # Probability increases as TTL approaches 0 delta = ttl - remaining_ttl probability = beta * delta / ttl
if random.random() < probability: # Refresh proactively — serve current, update in background try: data = loader() self.redis.setex(key, ttl, json.dumps(data)) return data except Exception: return json.loads(cached_data) # Serve stale on error
return json.loads(cached_data)
# Cache miss - load data data = loader() self.redis.setex(key, ttl, json.dumps(data)) return data
# Usage cache = EarlyRefreshCache(redis.Redis())
product = cache.get_with_refresh( key="product:123", loader=lambda: db.products.find_one({"_id": "123"}), ttl=3600, beta=1.5 # Refresh aggressively: starts refreshing around 33% TTL remaining )

section.name: request-coalescing
section.p: Request coalescing (single-flight) tracks in-flight requests by key and shares the result with all waiters. Unlike the distributed lock, coalescing operates within a single process and uses no Redis calls beyond the eventual cache population. It is the right choice for in-process L1/L2 caches and function-level memoization.

rule.statement: Request coalescing MUST set a timeout for the coalescing window (100–500ms). If the in-flight request does not complete within the timeout, waiting requests MUST fall through to their own fetch — never block indefinitely waiting for a coalesced result.
rule.rationale: An unbounded coalescing window turns a single slow origin request into a cascading queue of blocked requests. The coalescing timeout limits the blast radius: if the in-flight request stalls, waiters degrade to individual origin calls rather than an application-wide hang.

checklist.check: Identify cache keys with peak concurrency above 10 concurrent requests per second and origin load time above 100ms.
Select prevention strategy: distributed lock for cross-process, request coalescing for in-process, probabilistic refresh for read-heavy with soft staleness tolerance.
Set lock TTL to at least 3× the expected origin load time.
Implement fallback fetch() for all waiting requests in case the lock holder crashes or times out.
Add Prometheus counter for lock acquisitions, lock timeouts, and stampede-fallback events.
Load test the stampede scenario: flush the cache, fire 1000 concurrent requests, verify only one origin hit per key.
Use TTL jitter on repopulated keys to prevent the next synchronized expiry after a stampede recovery.

antipattern.description: Using SETNX then EXPIRE as two separate commands for distributed lock acquisition.
antipattern.reason: If the process crashes after SETNX but before EXPIRE, the lock key has no TTL and the lock is held indefinitely — permanent deadlock. The atomic SET NX EX command eliminates this race.

antipattern.description: Deleting the lock key without checking ownership (simple DEL instead of Lua check-and-delete).
antipattern.reason: If the lock TTL expires while the lock holder is still loading data, another process reacquires the lock. When the original holder finishes and calls DEL, it silently releases the new owner's lock, allowing a second concurrent rebuild — defeating the entire stampede guard.

antipattern.description: No fallback when a lock acquisition fails: returning an error response instead of serving stale data or falling through to origin.
antipattern.reason: A cache layer that returns errors during the stampede window amplifies the incident instead of degrading gracefully. Always serve stale or fall through to origin as a last resort.
